metadata.description: New vocabulary for everyday couple life — talking about work, food, exercise, weather, TV, and your cat

content.name: cat
content.side_e: Wǒmen de māo yòu zài shuì jiào le.
content.side_g: 小猫 (xiǎo māo) means 'kitten.' 猫咪 (māomī) is an affectionate term like 'kitty.' 喂猫 (wèi māo) means 'feed the cat.' 猫粮 (māo liáng) means 'cat food.' You can talk about your cat with locals — Chinese people love cats. 吸猫 (xī māo) is slang for 'cat cuddling.'

content.name: weather
content.side_e: Jīntiān tiānqì zěnmeyàng? Hǎo rè a!
content.side_f: How's the weather today? So hot!
content.side_g: 天 means 'sky/day' and 气 means 'air/atmosphere.' 天气很好 means 'the weather is nice.' 天气预报 (tiānqì yùbào) is 'weather forecast.' Great small talk opener with locals or when planning outdoor activities with your wife.

content.name: get_off_work
content.side_a: To get off work; to finish work
content.side_e: Nǐ jǐ diǎn xià bān? Wǒ yǐjīng xià bān le.
content.side_f: What time do you get off work? I already got off work.
content.side_g: 下 means 'down/off' and 班 means 'shift/class.' 下班了 (xià bān le) means 'done with work.' A very common daily phrase. Opposite is 上班 (shàng bān, 'go to work'). 你下班了吗？(Nǐ xià bān le ma?) is a common evening greeting between couples.

content.name: go_to_work
content.side_a: To go to work; to start work
content.side_e: Wǒ míngtiān bù shàng bān, wǒmen qù wánr ba!
content.side_f: I don't work tomorrow — let's go have fun!
content.side_g: 上 means 'up/go to' and 班 means 'shift.' 上班族 (shàngbānzú) means 'office worker/white collar.' 不上班 means 'day off/not working.' Ask locals about work: 你在哪儿上班？(Nǐ zài nǎr shàng bān? 'Where do you work?').

content.name: wife_casual
content.side_e: Wǒ lǎopo shuō xiǎng chī huǒguō.
content.side_f: My wife says she wants to eat hot pot.
content.side_g: 老婆 is the everyday/affectionate way to say 'wife' — used between friends and in casual conversation. More intimate than 太太. 老公 (lǎogōng) is the casual word for 'husband.' Chinese people will find it charming if you use 老婆 naturally.

content.name: cook
content.side_e: Jīntiān wǎnshang shéi zuò fàn? Wǒ lái zuò ba.
content.side_f: Who's cooking tonight? I'll do it.
content.side_g: 做 means 'to do/make' and 饭 means 'food/rice.' 做饭 is the general term for cooking at home. 做什么菜？(Zuò shénme cài?) means 'what are you cooking?' 会做饭 (huì zuò fàn) means 'can cook.' A classic couple conversation starter.

content.name: takeout_delivery
content.side_e: Bù xiǎng zuò fàn, wǒmen diǎn wàimài ba.
content.side_f: Don't feel like cooking — let's order delivery.
content.side_g: 外 means 'outside' and 卖 means 'sell.' Food delivery is huge in China — apps like 美团 (Měituán) and 饿了么 (È le me, literally 'Are you hungry?'). 点外卖 (diǎn wàimài) means 'order delivery.' 外卖到了 (wàimài dào le) means 'the delivery arrived.'

content.name: coffee
content.side_e: Zǎoshang wǒ yào hē yì bēi kāfēi.
content.side_f: I need to drink a cup of coffee in the morning.
content.side_g: Transliteration from English. 咖啡店 (kāfēi diàn) or 咖啡馆 (kāfēi guǎn) means 'coffee shop.' China has a massive coffee culture now — Luckin Coffee (瑞幸咖啡) is everywhere. 冰咖啡 (bīng kāfēi) is 'iced coffee.' 一杯咖啡 (yì bēi kāfēi) is 'a cup of coffee.'

content.name: exercise_work_out
content.side_e: Wǒ měitiān dōu duànliàn. Nǐ ne?
content.side_f: I exercise every day. What about you?
content.side_g: The general word for working out or physical exercise. 锻炼身体 (duànliàn shēntǐ) means 'exercise the body' — a complete phrase. 去锻炼 means 'go work out.' 健身 (jiànshēn) is more specifically 'fitness training.' Chinese people will be impressed if you talk about your workout routine.

content.name: run_jog
content.side_e: Wǒ zǎoshang qù pǎo bù le, pǎo le wǔ gōnglǐ.
content.side_f: I went running this morning — ran five kilometers.
content.side_g: 跑 means 'to run' and 步 means 'step.' 跑步 is specifically jogging/running for exercise. 去跑步 means 'go for a run.' 跑步机 (pǎobùjī) is 'treadmill.' 公里 (gōnglǐ) is 'kilometer' — China uses metric.

content.name: cold
content.side_e: Jīntiān hǎo lěng! Duō chuān yìdiǎn.
content.side_f: It's so cold today! Wear more layers.
content.side_g: Opposite of 热 (rè, 'hot'). 好冷 (hǎo lěng) means 'so cold.' 不冷不热 (bù lěng bú rè) means 'not cold not hot' — perfect weather. 冷死了 (lěng sǐ le) is an exaggeration meaning 'freezing to death.' Essential for weather talk.

content.name: happy_joyful
content.side_e: Jīntiān wǒ hěn kāixīn, yīnwèi lǎopo xià bān le!
content.side_f: I'm happy today because my wife got off work!
content.side_g: 开 means 'open' and 心 means 'heart' — literally 'open heart.' More colloquial and warm than 高兴 (gāoxìng). 不开心 (bù kāixīn) means 'unhappy.' 你开心吗？(Nǐ kāixīn ma?) is a warm way to ask 'are you happy?'

content.name: take_a_walk
content.side_a: To take a walk; to stroll
content.side_e: Chī wán fàn wǒmen qù sànbù ba.
content.side_f: After eating, let's go for a walk.
content.side_g: 散 means 'to scatter/relax' and 步 means 'step.' A very common after-dinner activity in China — you'll see crowds of people strolling after meals. 去散步 means 'go for a walk.' 散散步 (sànsanbù) is the relaxed reduplicated form.

content.name: bored
content.side_e: Hǎo wúliáo a, wǒmen kàn diànshìjù ba.
content.side_f: So bored — let's watch a TV show.
content.side_g: 无 means 'without' and 聊 means 'chat/interest.' Can describe a person (I'm bored) or a thing (it's boring). 太无聊了 (tài wúliáo le) means 'so boring.' Opposite: 有意思 (yǒu yìsi, 'interesting').

content.name: tv_show
content.side_f: What TV show are you watching recently?
content.side_g: 电视 means 'television' and 剧 means 'drama/play.' Chinese TV dramas (中国电视剧) are hugely popular. 美剧 (Měijù) means 'American TV show.' Great conversation topic: 你喜欢看什么电视剧？(What TV shows do you like watching?).
